vulnerability.title: Disable the use of user namespaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the user.max_user_namespaces kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: user.max_user_namespaces = 0 When containers are deployed on the machine, the value should be set to large non-zero value.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Disable the use of user namespaces

triage_decision.reason: The remediation involves setting a kernel sysctl parameter to 0 and persisting it via a file in /etc/sysctl.d. This change does not affect authentication, authorization, networking, or filesystem layout, and does not require a reboot. The primary risk is potential disruption of container workloads that rely on user namespaces, which can be mitigated by confirming container usage before applying the change.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Potential breakage of container workloads that depend on user namespaces; Reduced flexibility for applications that may need non-zero namespace limits

review_feedback: Setting user.max_user_namespaces to 0 disables user namespaces, which will break any workloads that rely on unprivileged containers (e.g., Podman, Docker, systemd-nspawn). While it will not lock out SSH or affect core services like auditd, firewalld, or networking, it can cause significant regression for systems that run containers, which may be considered critical. The plan does not verify whether containers are in use before applying the change.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly sets user.max_user_namespaces to 0 both at runtime and persistently, which addresses the vulnerability. It includes a pre‑check for existing settings and uses a high‑precedence sysctl.d file. Minor improvements can make it more robust.
concerns: If another sysctl.d file with a higher numeric prefix sets a different value, it could override the new setting.
The plan does not reload all sysctl files after creating the new config, relying only on the immediate sysctl -w command for runtime change.
suggested_improvements: After writing the new config file, run `sysctl --system` (or `sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-user-namespaces.conf`) to ensure all sysctl files are reloaded and the persisted value is applied.
Consider backing up any existing file that might already configure user.max_user_namespaces before creating the new one.
Document that kernels which require a minimum non‑zero value may reject setting 0, and note to test container workloads after applying the change.
